items.what_it_depicts: Autocar's hero photograph of the Bugatti Veyron from Steve Cropley's 2005 first-drive.
items.why_add: Primary source editorial photograph from the exhibit's main Veyron citation.

items.what_it_depicts: Autocar editorial photograph of the Veyron exterior from Cropley's first-drive feature.
items.why_add: Second Autocar editorial photograph from primary source.

items.what_it_depicts: Third Autocar editorial photograph of the Veyron.
items.why_add: Third editorial photograph from primary source.

items.what_it_depicts: Veyron chassis 5.0 — the validation prototype Ferdinand Piech personally signed off on in 2005 before series production.
items.why_add: The actual car Piech approved; anchors the 1,001 hp / 400 km/h narrative.
items.photographer_studio: Bugatti AG press archive via Supercars.net
items.chicago_citation: Bugatti AG, Bugatti Veyron Chassis 5.0 Validation Prototype, 2005, press archive via Supercars.net.

items.what_it_depicts: Chassis 5.0 — the car that ran the 407 km/h Ehra-Lessien validation with Uwe Novacki on April 19 2005.
items.why_add: Specific to the top-speed run referenced in the brief.
items.photographer_studio: Bugatti AG press archive via Supercars.net
items.chicago_citation: Bugatti AG, Bugatti Veyron Chassis 5.0, Ehra-Lessien Era, 2005.

items.what_it_depicts: Engineering detail of chassis 5.0 during 2005 development testing.
items.why_add: Shows the car during engineering validation — the development story.
items.photographer_studio: Bugatti AG press archive via Supercars.net
items.chicago_citation: Bugatti AG, Bugatti Veyron Chassis 5.0, Engineering Detail, 2005.

items.what_it_depicts: Profile view of chassis 5.0 — the prototype that enabled the Molsheim Atelier to open on September 3, 2005.
items.why_add: Visual anchor for the Molsheim Atelier opening moment.
items.photographer_studio: Bugatti AG press archive via Supercars.net
items.chicago_citation: Bugatti AG, Bugatti Veyron Chassis 5.0, Profile, 2005.

items.what_it_depicts: The Bugatti EB 18.3 Chiron concept, unveiled at the 1999 Frankfurt Motor Show — the direct precursor to the Veyron.
items.why_add: Fills the 1999 Frankfurt design-study gap; pre-Veyron design development.
items.chicago_citation: Scuderi Ferrari, Bugatti EB 18.3 Chiron Concept, 2007, Wikimedia Commons (Public Domain).

items.what_it_depicts: The Bugatti EB 18.4 Veyron concept, 1999 Tokyo Motor Show — first use of the 'Veyron' name on a Bugatti.
items.why_add: Critical naming-genealogy artifact; documents the 1999 Tokyo reveal.
items.chicago_citation: More Cars, Bugatti Veyron 18.4 Concept, 2025, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 4.0.

items.what_it_depicts: The Veyron's 8.0-litre quad-turbo W16 engine, open view.
items.why_add: Centerpiece of the engineering-story room; the 1,001 hp W16 in situ.
items.chicago_citation: Brian Snelson, Bugatti Veyron 8.0-litre W16 Engine, 2008, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 2.0.

items.what_it_depicts: The W16 engine installed on the Veyron chassis showing the packaging of four turbochargers and cooling system.
items.why_add: Shows the cooling-system-and-tire engineering story visually.
items.chicago_citation: Dane Poset, W16 Engine on a Bugatti Veyron Chassis, 2010, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0.

items.what_it_depicts: The Bugatti Veyron top-speed key — the mandatory second key that unlocks the 400 km/h mode by reconfiguring active aero.
items.why_add: Period artifact of the car's engineering protocol; visually distinctive, unique to the exhibit.
items.chicago_citation: EricS, Bugatti Veyron Top-Speed Key, October 19 2005, Wikimedia Commons (Public Domain).

items.what_it_depicts: Bugatti Veyron 16.4 Super Sport — the 1,200 hp variant that retook the speed record at 267 mph in 2010.
items.why_add: Fills the Super Sport variant coverage gap directly called out in the brief.
items.chicago_citation: Alexandre Prevot, Bugatti Veyron 16.4 Super Sport, 2020, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0.

items.what_it_depicts: Veyron 16.4 Super Sport World Record Edition — the specific edition commemorating the 267.8 mph top-speed run.
items.why_add: Anchors the Super Sport speed-record story visually.
items.chicago_citation: Alexandre Prevot, Bugatti Veyron 16.4 Super Sport World Record Edition, 2023, Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0.
